metadata.title: Topics in the Journal of Counseling Psychology, 1963–2015.

abstract:
Historical trends in a scientific field should be apparent in the changing content of journal articles over time. Using a topic modeling approach, a statistical method for quantifying the thematic content of text, 70 topics were extracted from the abstracts of 3,603 articles published in the Journal of Counseling Psychology from 1963 to 2015. After examining interpretability of 70 topics derived from the model, 64 meaningful topics and their trends were named. In addition, the authors also classified some of the related topics into 4 categories-counseling process and outcome, multiculturalism, research methodology, and vocational psychology. Counseling process and outcome related topics have decreased recently, while topics relating to multiculturalism and diversity have shown increasing trends. The authors also discussed trends that were observed and tried to account for the changing frequencies of some important research topics within these categories.

This study provides an illustration of how topical content in the Journal of Counseling Psychology has changed over time, providing a broad view of how the scientific focus of counseling psychology has evolved. In addition, it also demonstrates how text mining techniques can be used to analyze large amounts of documents in counseling psychology.

paragraphs: Research history is a record of the ongoing endeavors to extend what we already know to what we have not yet explored. It is important to remember that current inquiry is heavily dependent on what researchers have already achieved so far. On rare occasions, a scholar may singly have a large effect on a field without the comprehensive use of existing theories, contexts, and history. More commonly, progress in many fields is slow and incremental. There is no single isolated research-every research project is a steppingstone for subsequent inquiry, either by suggesting new possibilities or clarifying its own limitations. Without understanding this historical context, it is impossible to contribute meaningfully to a body of scholarship. Echoing Isaac Newton's famous quote, the fruit of fresh research findings can be reached only after standing on the shoulders of giants.

A flagship journal of an academic field is one of the primary sources of research history. Of course, one should not interpret it as a complete picture of a field's research history. For instance, articles published in a flagship journal may not be a pure reflection of a field's research trend, as there are numerous influences on what such a flagship journal may publish. Some of these influences include (a) the number of articles submitted, (b) page limitation, and (c) an editor's selective focus on specific issues. In addition, it should be also noted that there are various sources that reflect a field's identity and efforts other than journals (e.g., conferences, task forces, etc.). Nonetheless, there is no doubt that research trends in a flagship journal mirror researchers' collective efforts to advance our understanding of the field. For this reason, the Journal of Counseling Psychology (JCP), the flagship journal of counseling psychology, provides a rich set of reflections about the history of counseling psychology. It is one of the leading journals incorporating and facilitating a variety of scientific endeavors in this field since its beginning in 1954. The web page of JCP specifies that the JCP publishes empirical articles on "counseling activities," "career development and vocational psychology," "diversity and underrepresented populations in relation to counseling activities," "the development of new measures to be used in counseling activities," and "professional issues in counseling psychology," which covers some of the main interests that counseling psychologists have been working on (http://www.apa.org/pubs/journals/cou/). The web page also indicates that the JCP is interested in work about less severely disturbed populations and clients who are going through developmental crises, with consideration on the strengths and healthy traits of clients. These emphases on the journal's identity correspond to core values of counseling psychology suggested by Packard (2009), who cited "healthy development across the life span" and "strength, resilience, and positive coping" (p. 622) as values which distinguish counseling psychology from other professional areas. Considering the JCP's position as a flagship journal and its dedication to counseling psychology research, investigating research trends in the JCP can yield valuable perspectives on the research history of counseling psychology in general.

Content analysis is one method for providing an indication of the trends in topical foci of a journal. It is a useful technique to classify and summarize the characteristics of the journal, which improves our understanding of its contents. Several scholars have conducted content analyses previously to examine the content themes of the JCP, since its inception in 1954. For example, Munley (1974) performed a content analysis on the articles published in the first 19 volumes of JCP. By classifying the content themes of the articles into 17 different categories, he argued that no remarkable trends appeared during this period. Buboltz, Miller, and Williams (1999) and Buboltz, Deemer, and Hoffmann (2010) also investigated the JCP using a content analysis. Buboltz et al. (1999) analyzed the articles from 1974 to 1998, which covered a subsequent period of Munley's (1974) analysis, and reported some changes in publication trends and patterns. Articles related to vocational behavior and psychometric properties were less frequent in the given period (from 18% to 9% for the former, from 17% to 9% for the latter) even though these declines were not statistically significant, while the proportion of counseling process and outcome research remained relatively stable over time (around 25%). Buboltz et al. (2010) carried out a follow-up study 11 years later, based on articles from 1999 to 2009. During that period, the five most popular categories researched in the JCP were multiculturalism, development and evaluation of measures, personality and adjustment, counseling outcome research, and interpersonal/social support/attachment, accounting for 57% of the total articles. Although an increase in multicultural research and decrease in process and outcome research are notable, Buboltz et al. (2010) concluded that relatively consistent publication patterns were observed during this period. These three studies (Munley, 1974;Buboltz et al., 1999;Buboltz et al., 2010) covered most of the periods from its inception, which provided a useful summary for understanding the research trends in counseling psychology.

Despite the usefulness of content analysis, its practicality to guide research activity is somewhat limited because of its inevitable oversimplification. For instance, the previous content analyses reported increases and decreases in the frequency of counseling process and outcome research. Although content analysis can provide an idea about changing trends in this broadly defined topic, it does not give information on what kind of specific themes (e.g., working alliance) have emerged or disappeared within counseling process and outcome research. Researchers, especially those who are not familiar with research history, can benefit from a more detailed depiction of the field's research trends.

It is a challenging task to provide a more sophisticated overview of how research trends in counseling psychology have changed so far. One of the reasons for this challenge is the sheer amount of research that has accumulated over time. For instance, Web of Science retrieves 4,579 articles published in the JCP since its inception (as of August 27, 2016). It would require an enormous amount of time and effort to analyze these thousands of articles using more detailed content analysis approaches that involve first reading, then classifying contributions. The diverse interests of counseling psychology is another source of difficulty. As Ivey (1979) mentioned, counseling psychology is "the most broadly-based applied psychology specialty" (p. 3). This holds true for the research as well, as indicated by the broad research areas that JCP covers. It is likely that research history of counseling psychology is more complicated than that of other academic fields.

Considering these challenges of investigating research history in detail, an attractive alternative is to utilize statistical techniques to analyze the contents of the JCP, as it is much more efficient compared to examining articles by human judgment. Wampold and White (1985) provided a good example of this approach. Using a cluster analysis, Wampold and White (1985) examined 27 studies from the "Counseling Process and Outcome" sections of JCP issued in 1982, concluding that the social influence model was the most popular theme during the period studied. Cluster analysis was used in a more advanced way by Kivlighan and Miles (2007), by combining latent semantic analysis with a cluster analysis to derive six content themes from 72 articles of Group Dynamics: Theory, Research, and Practice from 1997 to 2002. A couple of different approaches utilizing quantitative analysis were also used to investigate the research trends of noncounseling psychology journals (e.g., Kevork & Vrechopoulos, 2009;Mela, Roos, & Deng, 2013). All of these studies were able to generate valuable and encompassing perspectives on research history. This suggests that a similar approach would be worthwhile in analyzing the rich history of JCP.

For this purpose, the current study investigated the changing trends of research topics in the JCP for the past 53 years , using a technique called topic modeling. Topic modeling allows researchers to extract main themes from large amounts of documents, and it has been applied to analyze journal contents in other academic fields (e.g., Wang, Bendle, Feng, & Cotte, 2015). For the current paper, we also categorized some of the relevant topics to discuss how our results are consistent with previous literature, hoping to provide a better understanding on research history of counseling psychology.

paragraphs: Topic modeling is an algorithmic approach for determining the thematic content of documents (Blei, 2012). Topic modeling has been applied to analyze various kinds of documents, such as press releases (Grimmer, 2010), newspapers (Rubin, Chambers, Smyth, & Steyvers, 2012), and social medias (Hong & Davison, 2010). Although topic modeling has not been widely used in the fields related to counseling psychology, a few researchers have explored the structure of psychotherapy transcripts using a topic modeling approach (e.g., Atkins et al., 2012;Imel, Steyvers, & Atkins, 2015).

Our approach to topic modeling relied upon latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA; Blei, Ng, & Jordan, 2003), the seminal topic model. One of the most basic assumptions of LDA is a "bag of words" assumption, which means the sequence of words within a document does not matter. Though this is an unrealistic assumption, it makes the computation process much simpler while still allowing us to explore semantic structure of documents. The goal of topic modeling is to determine the range of latent themes present in a collection of documents using the observed data of terms that occur within each document. LDA assumes that the thematic content of any given document is represented by the mixture of topics present within the document where each topic is defined as a mixture of constituent descriptive terms that mark or This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

indicate the presence of the topics (as given by words that appear within the document). LDA further assumes that all of the documents in a collection can be characterized by some combination of the range of topics identified in the analysis of a corpus. Mathematically, LDA estimates the following conditional probability distribution:

where ␤ is the distribution of topics in the collection of D documents (i.e., a corpus), K is the number of topics, represents the proportion of topics in a document, z is the assignment of words to topics, and w represents the observed words in the collection of documents. LDA uses the observed relationships of words within documents to estimate the range of thematic topics that exist, along with the relationships of each term to the topic and the extent to which each document possesses one or more of the latent topics. By doing so, it shows the topical composition of each document and also the semantic composition of topics (i.e., loadings of descriptive terms on each topic, similar to output from a factor analysis, Blei, 2012).

Figure 1 illustrates how topic modeling analyzes an actual document, which was one of the abstracts used in this study. According to the "bag of words" assumption, the sequence of words in this abstract does not matter. After associating the terms occurring in each document in a large set of data (in this case, thousands of abstracts), the topic model infers a hidden structure (i.e., topic structure) that is most likely to generate the given data (i.e., appearance of terms in any given abstract). Based on this topic structure, the abstract presented in Figure 1 can be understood as a mixture of five topics (for visual simplicity, only three most salient topics were represented in the figure). Notice that the labels are defined later by human judgment. As it can be seen in this example, a topic model can capture the semantic composition of documents without going through each abstract manually. Considering that this study's goal is at providing a general overview on the journal's research history, topic modeling is a sufficiently reliable tool for our purposes.

LDA uses the observed relationships of words within documents to estimate the range of thematic topics that exist, along with the relationships of each term to the topic and the extent to which each document possesses one or more of the latent topics. By doing so, it shows the topical composition of each document and also the semantic composition of topics (i.e., loadings of descriptive terms on each topic, similar to output from a factor analysis, Blei, 2012).

Figure 1 illustrates how topic modeling analyzes an actual document, which was one of the abstracts used in this study. According to the "bag of words" assumption, the sequence of words in this abstract does not matter. After associating the terms occurring in each document in a large set of data (in this case, thousands of abstracts), the topic model infers a hidden structure (i.e., topic structure) that is most likely to generate the given data (i.e., appearance of terms in any given abstract). Based on this topic structure, the abstract presented in Figure 1 can be understood as a mixture of five topics (for visual simplicity, only three most salient topics were represented in the figure). Notice that the labels are defined later by human judgment. As it can be seen in this example, a topic model can capture the semantic composition of documents without going through each abstract manually. Considering that this study's goal is at providing a general overview on the journal's research history, topic modeling is a sufficiently reliable tool for our purposes.
sections.preview_head: Topic modeling is an algorithmic approach for determining the thematic content of documents (Blei, 2012). Topic modeling has been applied to analyze various kinds of documents, such as press releases (Grimmer, 2010), newspapers (Rubin, Chambers, Smyth, & Steyvers, 2012), and social medias (Hong & Davison, 2010). Although topic modeling has not been widely used in the fields related to counseling psychology, a few researchers have explored the structure of psychotherapy transcripts using a topic modeling approach (e.g., Atkins et al., 2012;Imel, Steyvers, & Atkins, 2015).

sections.paper_id: Oh et al. - 2017 - Topics in the Journal of Counseling Psychology, 1963–2015.
paragraphs: The data used for this research came from the abstracts of articles published in the JCP from 1963 to 2015. The issues from 1954 to 1962 were excluded from the analysis because electronic copies of the article abstracts were not available and because the abstracts listed in the American Psychological Association (APA) PsycNET database were not rich enough to conduct analysis in terms of their contents and length. Articles without abstracts, abstracts with fewer than 50 words, comments and replies to previous articles, corrections, errata, introduction to special issues, editorials, test reviews, and book reviews also were omitted from the data that were analyzed. Meanwhile, special issues were included for the analysis, unlike the previous content analyses of the JCP (Buboltz et al., 1999;Buboltz et al., 2010). We decided to include them because special issues emerge from journal editors' intentional efforts to focus upon a topical area at the time. As a result, a total of 3,603 abstracts were retrieved and used for this study. As this study does not involve any human subjects, we did not obtain approval from the Institutional Review Board.

sections.paper_id: Oh et al. - 2017 - Topics in the Journal of Counseling Psychology, 1963–2015.
paragraphs: Abstracts and metadata of the 3,603 articles were retrieved from the APA PsycNET database with the permission of APA. The words in the abstracts were lemmatized (e.g., changing plural form into singular form, past-tense verb into bare infinitive) using the program named TreeTagger (Schmid, 1994) to analyze different words with the same root as a single item. Lemmatization is a usual step in natural language data processing projects (Jockers, 2014;Manning & Schütze, 2001), and it has the effect of making the texts of a corpus more standard and regular ahead of the main analyses. After that, punctuation, numbers, Greek letters (e.g., ␣, ␤), infrequent words (i.e., words that appeared fewer than five times in the 3,603 abstracts) were removed from the data. Common stop words (e.g., prepositions, conjunctions, articles, and other common terms such as be, have, for) and high frequency words that appeared in the abstracts (e.g., conclusion, discuss, significant, study) were also deleted, because these prevailing (Lee, Choe, Kim, & Ngo, 2000) showing how topic modeling works. Each topic is represented by a different color (Topic 1 ϭ Family Issues, Topic 62 ϭ Race and Ethnicity, Topic 68 ϭ Measures and Measurement). For simplicity, two topics whose words only appeared twice or less in this abstract are not represented here. Also note that certain words are not assigned to any topic (either stop words or words which the model was unsure where to assign). See the online article for the color version of this figure. This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

words across documents can interfere with extracting thematically meaningful topics and decrease the degree of model fit. Preprocessed data were analyzed by mallet (an R wrapper for the Machine Learning for Language Toolkit; Mimno, 2013) and R package dfrtopics (Goldstone, 2016b). (Note that there are other packages available, such as topicmodels and lda. In this study, we used mallet and dfrtopics, as they are linked with the data visualization program, which will be explained later.). To ensure that the topic model performs well, we modified the number of topics to be used in the analysis. Deciding the number of topics is an important issue in topic modeling, because it affects a model's performance. In topic modeling, the number of topics is designated by a researcher prior to running the analysis, and there is no clear way to know the optimal number of topics in advance. There are several ways to evaluate a model's performance, such as calculating perplexity (Blei et al., 2003) or the harmonic mean method (Griffiths & Steyvers, 2004), which can give information on how well a model performs to the varying number of topics. However, some research evidence suggests that statistical methods can be inaccurate and cannot replace human judgment when it comes to deciding a model's interpretability, which is the main concern of this study (Chang, Gerrish, Wang, Boyd-Graber, & Blei, 2009;Jockers, 2013). Therefore, we conducted multiple analyses with the different number of topics (from 50 to 125) and checked how interpretable the results were in each case. When the number of topics was too small, the model results miss details about certain topics or semantically different themes were merged into the same topic. When the number of topics was too large, semantically redundant topics were generated or the same themes were divided into ostensibly different topics for reasons that were not readily apparent. After trying different number of topics, we ended up with setting the number of topics as 70. We found that 70 is the best possible number in differentiating thematically meaningful topics, while maintaining the consistency of the topics. Our decision to rely on human judgment can be also supported by the fact that we used hyperparameter optimization suggested by Wallach, Mimno, and McCallum (2009), which is reported to be robust to the changing number of topics.

After running the model, the researchers labeled each topic, based on its list of top words and the titles of top documents (articles), using the topic browser which will be introduced later. We also examined the abstracts of top documents to make sure whether the label represented the topic well. For those topics that are not interpretable according to the researchers' judgment, we did not put labels on them. Based on the draft the first author made by following the abovementioned procedure, the authors collectively reviewed and revised the topic labels. Through this process, 64 topics were labeled in the end.

sections.paper_id: Oh et al. - 2017 - Topics in the Journal of Counseling Psychology, 1963–2015.
paragraphs: Counseling process and outcome research is often defined in a slightly different way according to the purpose of a researcher. For example, Buboltz et al. (2010) made a distinction among "outcome research," "process research," and "process and outcome research" based on whether research presented counseling process or outcome measures. Hill, Nutt, and Jackson (1994) defined it even more narrowly by excluding analogue research from process, outcome, and process and outcome research. Meanwhile, Scheel et al. (2011) used a broader term counseling-related research, including not only process and outcome research in general, but also counselor training and supervision research as well. In this research, we used the term counseling process and outcome research in a broad sense, which may include narrowly defined process research, outcome research, process and outcome research, and analogue research. In addition, unlike Scheel et al. (2011), we did not include supervision related research in this category, following the definition used in other studies.

The most salient feature in this category is an overall downward tendency across the topics with time. Although some topics show a slightly increasing tendency (e.g., Topic 11, Topic 33) and Figure 3 exhibits a somewhat upward trend over the last 5 years, it is not enough to offset general descending trends in these topics.

The previous literature on counseling process and outcome research seem to support this finding. For instance, the proportion of "process research" and "process and outcome research" in the JCP increased during 1973-1998 compared to previous years (from 9% to 13%, 1% to 25%, respectively; Buboltz et al., 1999;Munley, 1974), but it decreased sharply during the next period from 1999 to 2009 (from 13% to 5% from 25% to 4%, respectively; Buboltz et al., 2010). Scheel et al. (2011) also reported the noticeable decline of counseling related articles in the JCP between 1979 and 2008, with the especially striking decrease of analogue studies. The reduced interest in counseling analogue studies was also pointed out by Hill and Corbett (1993).

The possible explanations of this diminishing trend were suggested by Scheel et al. (2011)-more rigorous methodological criteria of the journal, acceptance of common factor approaches over specific factor approaches, emergence of other important research areas, and presence of other publication outlets. Based on his experience as an editor of JCP, Mallinckrodt (2011) suspected that decreasing production of counseling process and outcome research is a main reason for the current trend, which might be attributable to difficulty in accessing data in clinical settings and decreased emphasis on counseling process research in graduate training. Alternatively, the failure to include practitioners in counseling process and outcome research could be another possibility to explain the phenomenon (Vachon et al., 1995). Regardless of the reason, it is important for researchers to keep thinking about how this trend impacts the field of counseling psychology. This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

In addition to the general declining trends in counseling process research, the changing tendencies within this research area are also noteworthy. One of the most drastic changes has occurred in Topic 12 (Social Influence Theory; note that Topic 55 [Counseling Analog Interview] is also partly related to social influence theory). Since the social influence model was first introduced by Strong (1968), it had been one of the main theoretical models of process research in the 1970s and 1980s, as Wampold and White (1985) pointed out. However, Figure 2 shows that it has been rarely discussed in the 21st century. Another interesting topic to observe is Topic 11 (Working Alliance). The popularity of this topic has been constantly increasing since the Working Alliance Inventory was developed by Horvath and Greenberg (1989), making it one of the most frequently cited articles in the JCP. With this increasing trend, Duan and Hill (1996) raised an appealing explanation that decreasing interest in empathy research might be attributable to the shift of interest from empathy to working alliance, which seems to be possible based on the decreasing trend of Topic 23 (Empathy).

Based on his experience as an editor of JCP, Mallinckrodt (2011) suspected that decreasing production of counseling process and outcome research is a main reason for the current trend, which might be attributable to difficulty in accessing data in clinical settings and decreased emphasis on counseling process research in graduate training. Alternatively, the failure to include practitioners in counseling process and outcome research could be another possibility to explain the phenomenon (Vachon et al., 1995). Regardless of the reason, it is important for researchers to keep thinking about how this trend impacts the field of counseling psychology. In addition to the general declining trends in counseling process research, the changing tendencies within this research area are also noteworthy. One of the most drastic changes has occurred in Topic 12 (Social Influence Theory; note that Topic 55 [Counseling Analog Interview] is also partly related to social influence theory). Since the social influence model was first introduced by Strong (1968), it had been one of the main theoretical models of process research in the 1970s and 1980s, as Wampold and White (1985) pointed out. However, Figure 2 shows that it has been rarely discussed in the 21st century. Another interesting topic to observe is Topic 11 (Working Alliance). The popularity of this topic has been constantly increasing since the Working Alliance Inventory was developed by Horvath and Greenberg (1989), making it one of the most frequently cited articles in the JCP. With this increasing trend, Duan and Hill (1996) raised an appealing explanation that decreasing interest in empathy research might be attributable to the shift of interest from empathy to working alliance, which seems to be possible based on the decreasing trend of Topic 23 (Empathy).
sections.preview_head: Counseling process and outcome research is often defined in a slightly different way according to the purpose of a researcher. For example, Buboltz et al. (2010) made a distinction among "outcome research," "process research," and "process and outcome research" based on whether research presented counseling process or outcome measures. Hill, Nutt, and Jackson (1994) defined it even more narrowly by excluding analogue research from process, outcome, and process and outcome research.

ing interests in racial/ethnic minority issues in JCP. Ponterotto (1988) reported that 5.7% of articles published in JCP between 1976 and 1986 involved racial/ethnic minority issues. A steep increase in the popularity of Topic 20 (Sexual Minority) is another notable phenomenon. According to Buhrke, Ben-Ezra, Hurley, and Ruprecht (1992), only 0.22% (2 among total 918) articles published in JCP from 1978 to 1989 focused on lesbian and gay issues. LGB identities and attitudes (Lee et al., 2013). Finally, Topic 38 (Psychological Distress and Perceived Discrimination) also exhibited a drastic change, which is consistent with the report of Lee et al. (2013) that the number of articles on discrimination published This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

in JCP has increased within the domain of gender, race/ethnicity, and sexual orientation. This increased attention to multicultural research is also reflected in several special sections published in the JCP (race/ethnicity in counseling psychological process and outcome, Volume 41, Issue 2, 1999; racial and ethnic identity, Volume 54, Issue 3, 2007; sexual minority, Volume 56, Issue 1, 2009).

Regarding the increasing trends in multicultural research, it should be noted that multicultural research does not appear to be a distinct area from other research domains. Lee et al. (2013) argued that a large proportion of multicultural research conducted before 2009 in JCP had focused on counseling process, refuting the claim that counseling process research may have been driven out by the emergence of other research areas (Scheel et al., 2011;Mallinckrodt, 2011). This argument clearly pointed out that multicultural considerations are not isolated from more traditional research interests of counseling psychology. Foreman (1966), in his article reviewing publication trends in counseling journals, reported that JCP had shown dramatic increase in empirically based studies over theoretical articles during the period 1954 through 1965. Since then, JCP has emphasized the empirical basis of counseling research and stressed the importance of rigorous research methodology. Therefore, it is important to review the trends of the topics related to research methodology in the JCP.

sections.paper_id: Oh et al. - 2017 - Topics in the Journal of Counseling Psychology, 1963–2015.
paragraphs: The overall trends of the topics in this category can be summarized best with the term diversification. As Hayes and Kenney (1983) reported, various experimental designs were the most commonly used methodological design in the research published in the JCP from 1954 to 1980. Researchers criticized this dominance of an experimental approach, arguing that not every question in counseling psychology can be answered through experimental research (Goldman, 1976;Polkinghorne, 1984). They further proposed that research in counseling psychology pursue methodological diversity instead of uncritically accepting methodologies and philosophies from the physical sciences. Several decades later, we can now say that counseling psychology research has indeed become more diversified. As shown by Figure 2, the overall prevalence of Topic 66 and 32 (Experimental Design and Group Intervention) has decreased since the 1980s, even though it seems to be slightly increasing recently. Instead of experimental design research, other methodologies fill in the gap. Hoyt and Mallinckrodt (2012), based on their experiences as an associate editor and editor, respectively, of the JCP, stated that there seems to be increasing numbers of manuscripts using structural equation modeling (SEM) submitted to and published in JCP, which is consistent with the rising trend of Topic 27 (SEM and Mediation Model) in this study. Since Fassinger (1987) introduced SEM as a valuable method to counseling psychology research, the number of studies using this approach has increased. Frazier, Tix, and Barron (2004) also reported that SEM is one of the most frequently used methods to test moderator and mediator effects, along with multiple regression.

Another topic which shows an upward movement is Topic 58 (Qualitative Research Method). It was rarely discussed before the 1990s, but it is currently one of the main research methods to appear in the JCP. Having a special issue on qualitative research in 2005 (Volume 52, Issue 2) reflects this enhanced status of qualitative methods. In addition, the fact that Hill et al. (2005) becomes one of the most frequently cited articles in the JCP also supports this trend of increasing qualitative research designs within counseling psychology. Munley (1974) reported that 18% of the articles published in the JCP during 1954 through 1972 were about research on vocational behavior. Although the proportion of the research in this category has diminished since that time, it is still one of the main research areas that JCP is engaging in (9% for both 1973-1998and 1999-2009;Buboltz et al., 1999;Buboltz et al., 2010).

sections.paper_id: Oh et al. - 2017 - Topics in the Journal of Counseling Psychology, 1963–2015.
paragraphs: One of the main themes that appears in this research area is career assessment, reflected in Topic 59 (Vocational Inventories [Strong Vocational Inventory Blank, Strong-Campbell Interest Inventory, Kuder Occupational Interest Survey, and others]) and Topic 47 (Holland's Model and Vocational Interest). In the 1970s and 1980s, Strong Vocational Inventory Blank, Strong-Campbell Interest Inventory, and Kuder Occupational Interest Survey were the most popular tests to measure one's vocational interest. While these measures were rarely mentioned in current articles, Holland's RIASEC (Realistic, Investigative, Artistic, Social, Enterprising, and Conventional) model still seemed to attract researchers' attention until recently. Since Holland (1959) introduced this theory, it has constantly contributed to counseling psychology in an obvious way (Nauta, 2010;note that Topic 48 [Personality Measurement] is also partly related to Holland's RIASEC model). Even though it seems like Holland's theory was seldom discussed in the 2010s, it is too soon to evaluate its prevalence in the JCP based on this recent trend.

Topic 70 (Social Cognitive Career Theory) is another topic of interest. Since it was first proposed by Lent, Brown, and Hackett (1994), a fair amount of research using this theoretical framework has been published in the JCP. It offers an integrative way of understanding vocational behavior, allowing researchers to explore how career development can be understood through an interaction between personal factors and contextual variables (Lent, Brown, & Hackett, 2002). It also is noteworthy that there has been increasing attention to Topic 30 (Job/Life Satisfaction and Attribution Theory) in the past 5 to 10 years. It should be noted that topic 30 is mainly about job/life satisfaction, but attribution theory intrudes in it (attribution theory's influence is mainly concentrated in the 1970s and 1980s, and its impact on the topic is almost negligible after the 2000s).

The prevalence of these topics in the JCP seems to be consistent with the results of content analyses of other career-related journals. For example, Whiston, Rose, Peterson, and Nguyen (2013) reported that social cognitive career theory/self-efficacy theory and Holland's theory were the two most commonly used theories for the theoretically based articles in the Journal of Career Assessment from 1993 to 2011. A similar result also appeared in the content analysis of articles published in The Career Development Quarterly in 2013 (Sampson et al., 2014). In addition, Sampson et al. (2014) listed job satisfaction as one of the concepts that were not emphasized in the past, yet are highlighted in recent years in The Career Development Quarterly.

Since Holland (1959) introduced this theory, it has constantly contributed to counseling psychology in an obvious way (Nauta, 2010;note that Topic 48 [Personality Measurement] is also partly related to Holland's RIASEC model).

sections.paper_id: Oh et al. - 2017 - Topics in the Journal of Counseling Psychology, 1963–2015.
paragraphs: Data visualization is an important part of reporting the results, especially for the kind of exploratory analyses we have conducted here. The most popular methods to visualize the trends in topics include presenting a list of the most frequent words and showing a graph on changing topic popularity. However, these visualization techniques are vulnerable to misunderstanding as it can be misleading to infer topics solely based on the top words and address single topics separately without considering other relevant topics (Goldstone & Underwood, 2012;Schmidt, 2012). Moreover, considering that one of the main benefits of topic modeling is understanding the characteristics of individual documents within the larger context of topics, it is not enough to summarize the data in limited space.

To overcome these limitations of data visualization, we used a topic-model browser developed by Andrew Goldstone (2016a) to interpret our data. This topic browser can be accessed at https:// js-oh.github.io/jcp-topic-browser/ (see Figure 4). It allows users to explore the data interactively, offering rich information about the details of the data. The browser also provides an intuitive graphical interface that reveals the relationship between topics, documents, and words. For instance, readers can easily see how each topic consists of a different set of word distributions and how each document is represented as a distribution of multiple topics. Furthermore, the Overview-Scaled section provides a twodimensional plot showing the relationship among topics, and the Overview-Stacked section contains a stacked area plot for the entire topics. We believe that this topic browser would be especially helpful for beginning researchers who may be less familiar with the research history of counseling psychology. For example, if someone is interested in a certain research topic but not aware of what has been done in this area, this topic browser can be a good starting point to explore the field's previous efforts.

sections.paper_id: Oh et al. - 2017 - Topics in the Journal of Counseling Psychology, 1963–2015.
paragraphs: This is the first article using a probabilistic topic model to analyze research trends in the JCP. Although this is a powerful method to understand seemingly disparate and unorganized studies over a long period of time, there are a number of limitations that should be considered. First, we used abstracts for data analysis instead of the articles themselves, because of the practical issues like data retrieval and text preprocessing. Even though abstracts are believed to represent the contents of full articles, there is no guarantee that their topic content completely reflects the mixture of topics contained in the full-length articles. Therefore, the validity of this study is limited to the extent that abstracts correspond topically to the content embodied in articles. However, considering that similar previous studies also used abstracts to analyze research topics and publication trends, the use of abstracts can be acceptable when full-length articles are not available (e.g., Kivlighan & Miles, 2007;Wang et al., 2015).

A second limitation comes from the possible subjectivity and arbitrariness in the naming of topics. Because topic modeling derives its topics without being supervised by a researcher, it does not involve subjectivity in the categorization process. However, labeling topics is dependent on human judgment, which can be biased.

Figure 4. A screenshot from the topic browser (https://js-oh.github.io/jcp-topic-browser/). In this topic browser, topics, words, and documents were interactively organized so that users can easily explore the data. For instance, this web page shows that the abstract of Moradi, Dirks, and Matteson (2005) is represented as 77.0% of Topic 7 and 23.0% of Topic 27. A clickable link to each topic provides more detailed information on the topic structure. In addition, the abstract can be accessed through the link toward APA PsycNET. See the online article for the color version of this figure. This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

In addition, one needs to be cautious when interpreting this study's result as a complete history of counseling psychology. JCP is one of the most important outlets of the field's research activity, but it is only a part of the whole picture. As Scheel et al. (2011) appropriately pointed out, the output of a flagship journal can be influenced by other journals specialized in a specific area, such as Journal of Multicultural Counseling and Development, Journal of Career Development, and so on. Therefore, readers could benefit by complementing this study's finding with the research history of other related sources.

Another limitation of the current study is that LDA does not consider the change of topics over time. For example, top words used to represent a topic in 1963 may look significantly different from top words expressing the same topic in 2015, but LDA ignores this change. This study used standard LDA as its software is more accessible and efficient, but future research may utilize more advanced techniques like dynamic topic modeling (Blei, Lafferty, 2006) to capture the variable nature of a topic structure, especially over time.

More importantly, topic modeling is not a perfect method of revealing hidden topic structures of documents. In some areas, it may fail to yield a reasonable topic structure that is consistent with human judgment. For example, in some cases, certain topics might not be coherent, containing more than two distinctive themes in one topic. For instance, it is mentioned earlier that Topic 55 (labeled as Counseling Analogue Study) is partly related to social influence theory, which demonstrates the possible coexistence of two or more different themes in one topic. This phenomenon is more evident in Topic 30. The dominant theme of Topic 30 is job/life satisfaction, yet an obviously unrelated theme (attribution theory) intruded this topic. Another flaw of the model can be found in Topic 2 (Judgment). One of the main topical interests of counseling process research is clinical judgment. However, Topic 2 contains the articles about both clinical judgment and judgment used in other contexts (e.g., journal reviewer's judgment on manuscript). The same is also true for Topic 52 (Hypothesis Testing), failing to differentiate counselor's hypothesis testing strategies from hypothesis test in research context. Although topic modeling is generally sensitive in detecting how the same word is used in different contexts, this example shows that it might not be sensitive enough to entirely substitute human judgment.

Despite these limitations, we believe that this study can provide a broad yet detailed overview on the research trends of JCP. This study can be beneficial for those who want to submit their papers to the journal, as it gives an idea about popular research topics and the editorial board's preferences over time. Considering that JCP is the flagship journal of counseling psychology, this study may better inform us than the research history of a single journal. As publishing articles in a flagship journal is an influential way to establish the field's identity, changing trends in research topics can be conceptualized as a self-defining process of the field. Based on what we have found in this study, it is evident that counseling psychology refuses to define itself in a simple way. The field's pathway has become broader over time. It encompasses multiculturally diverse populations, tries to find a balance among various methodologies, and absorbs new theories and approaches to reform itself. This diversification can be either healthy or destructive. It can be seen as an either integrative and flexible approach or shattered and diffused identity. This is the nature of where our field brought us and we are the one who decides which direction this field will head to. By understanding these historical tensions to define what counseling psychology is, we will be able to connect our individual efforts with larger contexts. It is only after realizing this connection that we can push our field further beyond what we have already achieved so far.
